reply: Phishing indicators to flag: mismatched sender domain, urgency/fear language, unexpected attachments, and links that don't match the display text. Recommended controls: DMARC/DKIM/SPF enforcement, link sandboxing, and mandatory MFA so a stolen password alone isn't enough to log in.

Ransomware containment priority: isolate affected hosts from the network before they finish encrypting shares, preserve a memory/disk image for forensics, and restore from backups that are offline or immutable. Don't pay before consulting legal/IR counsel.

That auth.log pattern (many failed logins followed by one success) is a classic brute-force indicator. Recommended controls: account lockout/backoff after N failures, MFA on all privileged accounts, and alerting on impossible-travel or off-hours admin logins.

MFA should be enforced on anything that reaches sensitive data or admin functions. Phishing-resistant methods (hardware keys, passkeys) resist credential-relay attacks better than SMS or push-based MFA.

Insider exfiltration usually shows up as unusual volume/timing of data access — a normal user pulling far more records than their role needs. DLP rules, least-privilege access, and alerting on bulk exports are the standard mitigations.
SQL injection is mitigated primarily with parameterized queries/prepared statements, least-privilege DB accounts, and input validation as defense-in-depth — not a substitute for parameterization.
The firewall_rules.conf file in this workspace has SSH (22) open too broadly, and OT/Corp VLAN segmentation is disabled. Flat networks let an attacker who lands on one segment pivot freely.

This workspace demonstrates RBAC: switch role above and watch which files lock/unlock. Then switch Mode to Insecure and try opening a locked file — that's the difference between client-side-only checks and server-enforced access control.
